title: Cloudbursts and Orographic Lifting
focus: Definition, meteorological mechanisms (orographic lift), and disaster risk reduction strategies for cloudbursts.
rationale: This section covers a distinct meteorological phenomenon, detailing specific IMD criteria and the geographical mechanisms that cause it.
sectionExcerpt: Extreme precipitation in a short period, sometimes accompanied by hail/thunder, creating flood conditions. Occurs in plains as well, but more common in mountainous zones, due to orographic lift. India Meteorological Department (IMD) terms a rain event as cloud burst if 10 cm rainfall is received at a station in one hour. Challenges with Tackling: Climate Change (E.g., every 1°C rise lets air hold ~7% more moisture). Measures: Cloudburst Disaster Risk Reduction (DRR) Strategy – National Disaster Management Plan (2019) including Landslide Hazard Zonation (LHZ).
evidenceBullets: The IMD defines a cloudburst as an event where 10 cm of rainfall is received at a station in one hour.
Cloudbursts are more common in mountainous zones due to orographic lift, where moist air is forced to rise, cooling and condensing rapidly.
A violent upward flow of air prevents condensing raindrops from falling until the lack of upward air causes the whole stored water to fall at once.
Climate change exacerbates cloudbursts, as every 1°C rise in temperature allows the air to hold approximately 7% more moisture.

title: Glacial Lakes and Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs)
focus: Types of glacial lakes, causes of GLOFs, and India's monitoring and mitigation strategies.
rationale: Groups the formation of glacial lakes, the mechanics of outburst floods, and specific Indian monitoring frameworks (CWC, NGRMP) into a single coherent topic.
sectionExcerpt: Glacial lakes: Water formed by melting glaciers, usually at or near glacier's base. Two primary types: Supraglacial lakes (Formed by meltwater in depressions on glaciers) and Moraine-dammed lakes (Formed by meltwater at glacier toes, dammed by loose debris or ice-cores, prone to sudden failure). GLOFs: Flood resulting from sudden and rapid release of water from a glacial lake. Major Causes: Glacial Surging, Moraine Dam Failure, Seismic Activities. Central Water Commission (CWC) monitors 902 glacial lakes and water bodies in the Himalayas. National Glacial Lake Outburst Flood (GLOF) Risk Mitigation Project (NGRMP): In Arunachal Pradesh, Himachal Pradesh, Sikkim and Uttarakhand.
evidenceBullets: Supraglacial lakes form in depressions on glaciers, while moraine-dammed lakes form at glacier toes and are highly prone to sudden failure.
GLOFs can be triggered by glacial surging (rapid short-term advance of a glacier), moraine dam failure, seismic activities, and human activities.
The Central Water Commission (CWC) monitors 902 glacial lakes and water bodies in the Himalayas.
The National GLOF Risk Mitigation Project (NGRMP) covers the states of Arunachal Pradesh, Himachal Pradesh, Sikkim, and Uttarakhand.
Synthetic Aperture Radar imagery is utilized to automatically detect changes in the size of glacial lakes.

title: Tsunamis and NDMA Guidelines
focus: Causes of tsunamis, wave characteristics (wavelength, amplitude, shoaling), and NDMA mitigation guidelines.
rationale: Focuses on the physical characteristics of tsunami waves across different ocean depths and the institutional mitigation measures recommended by the NDMA.
sectionExcerpt: Series of extremely long waves caused by large and sudden displacement of ocean, usually result of an earthquake below or near ocean floor. Causes: Earthquakes, landslides, volcanic eruptions, meteorites, underwater explosions. Wavelength: Speed & wavelength primarily depends on ocean depth, not distance from wave's source. Wavelength in deep ocean is ~200 km and reduces to less than 20 km near coast due to shoaling. Wave Height (Amplitude): Not noticed by ships in deep oceans because their amplitude is negligible. When it approach shallow water, wave amplitude increases. NDMA Guidelines for Tsunami: Mandate a multi-tiered institutional structure... Developing Natural buffers like bio shields, mangrove regeneration etc.
evidenceBullets: Tsunamis can be caused by earthquakes, landslides, volcanic eruptions, meteorites, and underwater explosions.
The speed and wavelength of a tsunami primarily depend on ocean depth rather than the distance from the wave's source.
In deep oceans, tsunami wave amplitude is negligible and often goes unnoticed by ships.
As tsunami waves approach shallow coastal waters, their wavelength decreases due to shoaling, and their wave amplitude increases significantly.
NDMA guidelines recommend developing natural buffers such as bio-shields and mangrove regeneration to mitigate tsunami impacts.

title: Oil Spills and Marine Pollution Frameworks
focus: Hazards of chemical spills (Calcium Carbide), impacts of oil spills, and national/global regulatory frameworks.
rationale: Consolidates marine pollution incidents, including oil and chemical spills, with their corresponding legal and regulatory frameworks (MARPOL, BUNKER, NOSDCP).
sectionExcerpt: Calcium Carbide: A hazardous chemical that reacts violently with seawater to produce highly flammable acetylene gas. Use of calcium carbide for ripening fruits has been banned under Food Safety and Standards Regulations, 2011. Key Framework on Oil Spills: Merchant Shipping Act (1958) adopts MARPOL conventions. National Oil Spill Disaster Contingency Plan (1996): Indian Coast Guard coordinates oil spill response in India's maritime zones as a nodal agency. GLOBAL: MARPOL (1973): International convention preventing marine pollution from ships. BUNKER Convention (2001): Holds shipowners liable for bunker oil pollution. Impact: Poisons algae, disrupting food chains; Affect benthic organisms. E.g., clams and mussels.
evidenceBullets: Calcium carbide reacts violently with seawater to produce highly flammable acetylene gas and is banned for fruit ripening in India under FSSAI regulations.
The Indian Coast Guard acts as the nodal agency coordinating oil spill responses under the National Oil Spill Disaster Contingency Plan (1996).
The Merchant Shipping Act (1958) adopts MARPOL conventions for comprehensive maritime governance and pollution control in India.
The BUNKER Convention (2001) holds shipowners liable for bunker oil pollution and mandates compulsory insurance coverage.
Oil spills negatively impact benthic organisms like clams and mussels and poison algae, which disrupts marine food chains.
